topic: A musician hears their song being performed by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the cramped coffee shop thickened instantly. Elias, clutching his lukewarm latte, froze mid-sip. The melody—his melody, the one he’d poured six months of sleepless nights into—was spilling out of the acoustic guitar being played by a stranger at the corner table. His heart hammered against his ribs, a frantic, trapped bird. He squeezed his eyes shut for a fleeting second, a wave of intense heat rising in his cheeks. When he dared to look again, the performer was just a silhouette against the dusty window, utterly absorbed in the notes. Elias felt a sudden, desperate urge to melt into the worn upholstery, to simply cease existing in that moment. He fumbled for his phone, intending to call security, but his fingers felt clumsy, thick with sudden, overwhelming awkwardness.

[story 2]

I was supposed to be catching up on emails, but the sound cut through the silence of my apartment like a physical blow. It was my track, the one I’d been agonizing over for weeks, being sung with a raw, vibrant energy by someone I didn't recognize. A cold sweat broke out across my forehead. I stood rigid by the window, watching the street below, my breath catching in shallow gasps. Every nerve ending felt exposed, screaming under the weight of the realization. I wanted the floor to swallow me whole. Slowly, deliberately, I turned away from the window, focusing intently on a dust mote dancing in a shaft of sunlight, trying to anchor myself to something solid before the sheer audacity of the situation overwhelmed me.

[story 3]

The stage was small, dimly lit, and entirely unexpected. As the opening chords of my composition filled the room, I watched, paralyzed, as a completely unknown artist took the reins. The familiarity of the arrangement, the precise way the vocal runs were executed—it was mine. A deep flush spread from my neck up to my hairline, making my skin feel prickly and hypersensitive. I shifted my weight from one foot to the other, a nervous tic asserting itself. A low, involuntary sound escaped my throat—a choked little noise—and I immediately clamped my mouth shut, wishing the floor would open up and consume me whole. I couldn't look away, yet I couldn't bear to acknowledge the performance any further.
